tasks.title: Implement Lexer for Go Compatibility
tasks.description: Create a lexer that tokenizes Go source code with Goo extensions
tasks.details:
Implement a lexer that can tokenize all valid Go syntax plus Goo extensions including:
- Standard Go tokens (keywords, operators, literals)
- Goo-specific tokens: '!' for error unions, '?' for nullable types
- Channel pattern keywords (pub, sub, req, rep)
- 'comptime' keyword
- Ownership qualifiers

The lexer should:
1. Read source code from .goo files
2. Produce a token stream with position information
3. Handle all Go escape sequences
4. Support Unicode identifiers

Implementation approach:
```go
type TokenType int

const (
    // Standard Go tokens
    TOKEN_IDENT TokenType = iota
    TOKEN_INT
    TOKEN_FLOAT
    TOKEN_STRING
    // ... other Go tokens
    
    // Goo extensions
    TOKEN_BANG        // !
    TOKEN_QUESTION    // ?
    TOKEN_COMPTIME    // comptime
    TOKEN_PUB         // pub (channel pattern)
    TOKEN_SUB         // sub (channel pattern)
    TOKEN_REQ         // req (channel pattern)
    TOKEN_REP         // rep (channel pattern)
    // ... ownership tokens
)

type Token struct {
    Type    TokenType
    Literal string
    Pos     Position
}

type Lexer struct {
    input        string
    position     int
    readPosition int
    ch           byte
    line         int
    column       int
}

func NewLexer(input string) *Lexer {...}
func (l *Lexer) NextToken() Token {...}
```
tasks.testStrategy:
1. Unit test each token type recognition
2. Test with valid Go programs to ensure all syntax is recognized
3. Test with Goo extensions to verify new token types
4. Test error cases with invalid syntax
5. Test Unicode handling
6. Benchmark performance with large files
7. Compare output with Go's standard lexer for compatibility validation

tasks.title: Develop Parser for AST Generation
tasks.description: Create a parser that builds an Abstract Syntax Tree from the token stream
tasks.details:
Implement a recursive descent parser that converts the token stream from the lexer into an Abstract Syntax Tree (AST). The parser should:

1. Support all Go syntax (packages, imports, declarations, statements, expressions)
2. Add support for Goo extensions (error unions, nullable types, channel patterns)
3. Build a complete AST with position information for error reporting
4. Handle operator precedence correctly

Core AST structures:
```go
type Node interface {
    Pos() Position
    Type() Type  // Will be filled during type checking
}

type Program struct {
    Package     string
    Imports     []ImportSpec
    Declarations []Declaration
}

type FuncDecl struct {
    Name       string
    Params     []Param
    ReturnType Type  // Can be error union with !
    Body       []Stmt
    Attributes []Attr  // comptime, unsafe, etc.
}

type ChannelType struct {
    Pattern  ChannelPattern  // basic, pub, sub, req, rep
    ElemType Type
    Endpoint string  // Optional network endpoint
}
```

Implementation approach:
1. Create a Parser struct that takes a Lexer
2. Implement recursive descent parsing for each grammar rule
3. Use precedence climbing for expression parsing
4. Build appropriate AST nodes for each construct
5. Track and attach source positions to nodes
6. Add special handling for Goo extensions
tasks.testStrategy:
1. Unit test parsing of individual constructs (functions, types, expressions)
2. Test with complete Go programs to verify full syntax support
3. Test Goo extensions specifically
4. Test error recovery and reporting
5. Verify AST structure matches expected output
6. Test with malformed input to ensure robust error handling
7. Compare parsed structure with Go's standard parser for compatibility

tasks.title: Implement Type System and Type Checker
tasks.description: Create the type system with Goo extensions and implement type checking for the AST
tasks.details:
Implement the type system and type checker that enforces type safety, including Goo's ownership tracking, nullable types, and error unions.

Type System Components:
```go
type Type interface {
    String() string
    Size() int
    Align() int
}

type OwnershipKind int
const (
    Owned OwnershipKind = iota
    Borrowed
    Shared
)

type QualifiedType struct {
    Base      Type
    Nullable  bool
    Ownership OwnershipKind
}

type ErrorUnionType struct {
    ValueType Type
    ErrorType Type  // Usually a standard error type
}

type ChannelType struct {
    Pattern  ChannelPattern
    ElemType Type
}
```

Type Checker Implementation:
1. Create a TypeChecker struct that traverses the AST
2. Implement type inference for expressions
3. Verify type compatibility in assignments and function calls
4. Check ownership rules (simplified from Rust)
5. Enforce null safety with nullable types
6. Validate error union handling
7. Verify channel pattern usage

Ownership Checking:
- Track variable ownership through scopes
- Detect use-after-move errors
- Implement borrowing rules
- Generate clear error messages for ownership violations

Null Safety:
- Ensure nullable types are checked before use
- Track null state through control flow
- Prevent null pointer dereferences
tasks.testStrategy:
1. Unit test type checking for basic expressions
2. Test type inference in various contexts
3. Test ownership tracking with move semantics
4. Test nullable type safety enforcement
5. Test error union handling and propagation
6. Verify channel type compatibility
7. Test with intentional type errors to verify error reporting
8. Benchmark performance with large codebases

tasks.title: Develop LLVM IR Code Generator
tasks.description: Create a code generator that translates the typed AST to LLVM IR
tasks.details:
Implement a code generator that translates the typed AST into LLVM IR for compilation to native code. The code generator should:

1. Set up LLVM module, context, and builder
2. Generate LLVM types from Goo types
3. Generate function declarations and definitions
4. Translate expressions to LLVM instructions
5. Implement control flow (if, for, switch)
6. Handle function calls and returns
7. Support error unions and nullable types

LLVM Integration:
```go
type CodeGenerator struct {
    context  llvm.Context
    module   llvm.Module
    builder  llvm.Builder
    function llvm.Value
    typeMap  map[Type]llvm.Type
    valueMap map[string]llvm.Value
}

func NewCodeGenerator(moduleName string) *CodeGenerator {...}
func (g *CodeGenerator) GenerateProgram(program *Program) error {...}
func (g *CodeGenerator) GenerateFunction(funcDecl *FuncDecl) (llvm.Value, error) {...}
func (g *CodeGenerator) GenerateExpression(expr Expr) (llvm.Value, error) {...}
```

Special Handling:
1. Error unions: Generate code for error checking and propagation
2. Nullable types: Generate null checks before dereference
3. Ownership: Generate move/copy operations based on ownership
4. Channel operations: Generate runtime calls for channel operations

Optimization:
- Apply LLVM optimization passes
- Implement inlining for small functions
- Use LLVM's constant folding
tasks.testStrategy:
1. Unit test code generation for basic constructs
2. Test generated LLVM IR for correctness
3. Verify error union implementation
4. Test nullable type implementation
5. Test ownership tracking in generated code
6. Benchmark generated code performance
7. Compare with Go's code generation for compatibility
8. Test with various optimization levels

tasks.title: Implement Null Safety System
tasks.description: Implement nullable types with ? syntax and safe unwrapping
tasks.details:
Implement the null safety system with ? nullable types and safe unwrapping. This includes:

1. Extend the type system to support nullable types (?T)
2. Implement null checking before dereference
3. Add if-let syntax for safe unwrapping
4. Generate appropriate LLVM IR for null checks

Language Syntax:
```go
// Nullable variable declaration
var name: ?string = nil

// Safe unwrapping with if-let
if let actualName = name {
    // actualName is non-nullable string here
    fmt.Println("Hello, " + actualName)
} else {
    fmt.Println("Hello, anonymous")
}

// Forced unwrapping (will panic if nil)
fmt.Println("Hello, " + name!)
```

Implementation Details:
1. Modify the parser to recognize ? type syntax
2. Extend the type checker to handle nullable types
3. Implement if-let syntax in the AST
4. Generate LLVM IR for null checking
5. Create runtime support for null checking
6. Implement panic for forced unwrapping of nil values

The null safety system should prevent null pointer dereferences at compile time when possible, and add runtime checks when needed.
tasks.testStrategy:
1. Test parsing of nullable type syntax
2. Test type checking of nullable types
3. Verify if-let correctly unwraps nullable values
4. Test forced unwrapping with both nil and non-nil values
5. Verify compiler prevents unchecked nullable access
6. Test complex nullable type scenarios
7. Benchmark performance impact of null safety

tasks.title: Implement Data Parallel Processing
tasks.description: Add an OpenMP-like parallel programming model with parallel for loops, work distribution, and synchronization primitives to enable efficient data-parallel operations while maintaining memory safety.
tasks.details:
Implement a comprehensive data parallel processing system that builds on the existing goroutine infrastructure to provide OpenMP-like functionality:

1. Parallel For Loops:
   - Implement a `parallel for` construct that automatically distributes iterations across available threads
   - Support different scheduling strategies (static, dynamic, guided)
   - Allow specification of chunk sizes for work distribution
   ```go
   parallel for i := 0; i < len(array); i++ {
       // Work done in parallel
   }
   ```

2. Work Distribution Mechanisms:
   - Implement work-stealing queues for dynamic load balancing
   - Create thread pool management for efficient worker reuse
   - Support task chunking for granularity control

3. Shared Memory Synchronization:
   - Implement atomic operations (compare-and-swap, fetch-and-add)
   - Add mutex and read-write lock primitives
   - Provide memory fences/barriers for ordering operations
   ```go
   var counter atomic.Int
   counter.Add(1)
   ```

4. Reduction Operations:
   - Implement parallel reduction for common operations:
     - Sum, product, min, max
     - Custom user-defined reductions
   ```go
   sum := parallel reduce(array, 0, func(a, b int) int { return a + b })
   ```

5. Thread-Local Storage:
   - Implement thread-local variables for per-thread data
   - Provide efficient access patterns for thread-local data
   ```go
   threadLocal var counter int
   ```

6. Barrier Synchronization:
   - Add barrier primitives for synchronizing parallel sections
   - Support named barriers for complex synchronization patterns
   ```go
   barrier()  // All threads must reach this point before any proceed
   ```

7. Integration with Ownership System:
   - Extend the type system to track data access patterns in parallel regions
   - Implement compile-time checks for data race detection
   - Add runtime verification for dynamic parallel patterns
   - Ensure memory safety guarantees are maintained during parallel execution

8. Runtime Support:
   - Extend the runtime system to manage thread pools
   - Implement work distribution algorithms
   - Add performance monitoring and tuning capabilities

The implementation should focus on both performance and safety, ensuring that the parallel constructs integrate seamlessly with Goo's ownership system to prevent data races while maintaining high performance.
tasks.testStrategy:
1. Unit Testing:
   - Create unit tests for each parallel primitive (for loops, reductions, barriers)
   - Test different scheduling strategies with varying workloads
   - Verify correct results for reduction operations with different data types
   - Test thread-local storage with multiple concurrent accesses

2. Race Detection:
   - Implement test cases with intentional race conditions to verify detection
   - Create boundary tests that push the limits of the ownership system
   - Verify that safe parallel patterns compile while unsafe ones are rejected
   - Test dynamic race detection for patterns that can't be verified at compile time

3. Performance Testing:
   - Benchmark parallel operations against sequential equivalents
   - Measure scaling efficiency with increasing thread counts
   - Compare performance against equivalent Go code using goroutines
   - Test with different workload characteristics (compute-bound, memory-bound)

4. Integration Testing:
   - Create end-to-end tests that use parallel constructs in realistic scenarios
   - Test interaction between parallel regions and other language features
   - Verify correct behavior when exceptions/errors occur in parallel regions

5. Stress Testing:
   - Run parallel operations with extreme parameters (very large arrays, many threads)
   - Test system behavior under resource constraints
   - Verify stability during long-running parallel operations

6. Example Programs:
   - Implement classic parallel algorithms (matrix multiplication, sorting)
   - Create data processing pipelines using the parallel constructs
   - Build a small benchmark suite demonstrating different parallel patterns

7. Validation Suite:
   - Create a comprehensive validation suite that verifies:
     - Correctness of results across different thread counts
     - Absence of data races in safe code
     - Detection of unsafe patterns
     - Performance scaling with problem size and thread count

tasks.title: Implement Pattern Matching
tasks.description: Add match expressions with exhaustiveness checking, pattern compilation, and guard conditions for safe and ergonomic control flow.
tasks.details:
Implement pattern matching as a first-class language feature that provides a more powerful and safer alternative to switch statements. The implementation should include:

1. AST Extensions:
   - Create new AST node types for match expressions, patterns, and guards
   - Define pattern types: literal patterns, variable bindings, destructuring patterns, type patterns, and wildcard patterns

2. Pattern Compilation:
   - Implement a pattern compiler that translates patterns into efficient runtime checks
   - Support nested patterns for complex data structures
   - Implement destructuring for structs, arrays, slices, and custom types

3. Exhaustiveness Checking:
   - Implement static analysis to verify all possible cases are handled
   - Generate compile-time errors for non-exhaustive matches
   - Consider type information to determine possible variants

4. Guard Conditions:
   - Support boolean expressions as additional filters on patterns
   - Implement scoping rules for variables bound in patterns and used in guards

5. Type Refinement:
   - Track type narrowing through pattern matching
   - Update the type environment for the scope of each match arm

Example syntax:
```go
match value {
  case 0:
    fmt.Println("Zero")
  case n if n < 0:
    fmt.Println("Negative:", n)
  case n if n > 0 && n < 10:
    fmt.Println("Small positive:", n)
  case n:
    fmt.Println("Large positive:", n)
}

match err {
  case nil:
    // Success case
  case io.EOF:
    // Specific error handling
  case e:
    // Default error case
}

match obj {
  case Person{Name: name, Age: age} if age > 18:
    fmt.Println(name, "is an adult")
  case Person{Name: name}:
    fmt.Println(name, "is a minor")
  case _:
    fmt.Println("Not a person")
}
```

Integration with the type system:
- Leverage the existing type system to determine possible variants for exhaustiveness checking
- Ensure pattern matching works with all Goo type extensions (nullable types, error unions)
- Implement special handling for error unions to encourage proper error handling

Performance considerations:
- Compile patterns to efficient decision trees or jump tables where possible
- Minimize runtime overhead for pattern evaluation
- Optimize common patterns (like enum matching) for better performance
tasks.testStrategy:
1. Unit Tests:
   - Create unit tests for each pattern type (literals, variables, destructuring, etc.)
   - Test exhaustiveness checking with various type combinations
   - Verify guard conditions work correctly with different expressions
   - Test error cases (non-exhaustive matches, invalid patterns)

2. Integration Tests:
   - Test pattern matching with complex data structures
   - Verify interaction with the type system, especially with nullable types and error unions
   - Test compilation of match expressions to ensure correct code generation
   - Benchmark performance against equivalent switch statements

3. Specific Test Cases:
   - Match against primitive types (integers, strings, booleans)
   - Match against composite types (structs, arrays, slices)
   - Match with type patterns and type assertions
   - Match with nested patterns and complex destructuring
   - Match with guard conditions of varying complexity
   - Match against error unions and nullable types
   - Edge cases: empty match statements, unreachable patterns

4. Compiler Tests:
   - Verify correct error messages for non-exhaustive matches
   - Test that the compiler correctly identifies unreachable patterns
   - Ensure proper scoping of variables bound in patterns

5. Documentation Tests:
   - Create examples demonstrating all pattern matching features
   - Document best practices and common patterns
   - Provide performance guidelines for efficient pattern matching

tasks.title: Implement Unsafe Blocks & Hardware Control
tasks.description: Add unsafe syntax and semantics to the language, including raw pointer operations, inline assembly support, FFI capabilities, and hardware control primitives for LughOS integration.
tasks.details:
This task involves implementing unsafe features that allow low-level system programming while maintaining the language's safety guarantees elsewhere:

1. Unsafe Block Syntax:
   - Implement `unsafe` keyword and block syntax
   - Create parser rules for unsafe blocks in the AST
   - Add type checking rules specific to unsafe contexts
   - Ensure unsafe operations are only allowed within unsafe blocks

2. Raw Pointer Operations:
   - Implement pointer type syntax (e.g., `*unsafe.Pointer`)
   - Add pointer arithmetic operations (+, -, [], etc.)
   - Implement pointer casting between different types
   - Support dereferencing operations with proper bounds checking options

3. Inline Assembly Support:
   - Create syntax for inline assembly blocks (e.g., `asm { ... }`)
   - Support different assembly dialects (AT&T, Intel)
   - Implement operand constraints and clobber lists
   - Generate appropriate LLVM inline assembly instructions

4. Foreign Function Interface (FFI):
   - Implement C ABI compatibility layer
   - Add support for `extern "C"` function declarations
   - Create mechanisms for marshaling data between Goo and C types
   - Support dynamic library loading and symbol resolution

5. Hardware Control Primitives:
   - Implement memory-mapped I/O operations
   - Add port I/O instructions (in/out on x86)
   - Support interrupt handling mechanisms
   - Implement atomic operations for hardware synchronization

6. Kernel-Specific Memory Management:
   - Add page table manipulation primitives
   - Implement physical memory allocation interfaces
   - Support for memory protection and permission settings
   - Create safe abstractions around unsafe memory operations

7. LLVM IR Generation:
   - Extend the LLVM IR code generator to handle unsafe operations
   - Generate appropriate LLVM intrinsics for hardware operations
   - Implement inline assembly translation to LLVM IR
   - Ensure proper optimization barriers around volatile operations

8. Safety Mechanisms:
   - Implement compile-time warnings for unsafe code usage
   - Add runtime checks at unsafe/safe boundaries where possible
   - Create linting tools to identify potentially dangerous patterns
   - Document best practices for using unsafe features safely

Code examples:

```go
// Raw pointer operations
unsafe {
    ptr := (*int)(unsafe.Pointer(uintptr(0x1000)))
    *ptr = 42
    ptr = ptr + 1  // pointer arithmetic
}

// Inline assembly
unsafe {
    asm {
        "mov $42, %eax"
        "ret"
        : "=a"(result)
        :
        : "memory"
    }
}

// Memory-mapped I/O
unsafe {
    mmioReg := (*volatile uint32)(unsafe.Pointer(uintptr(0xFFFF0000)))
    *mmioReg = 0x1  // Write to hardware register
    value := *mmioReg  // Read from hardware register
}

// Port I/O (x86)
unsafe {
    outb(0x64, 0xFE)  // Send reset command to keyboard controller
}
```
tasks.testStrategy:
Testing unsafe features requires a comprehensive approach across multiple levels:

1. Syntax and Parsing Tests:
   - Create unit tests for parsing unsafe blocks and related syntax
   - Verify AST correctly represents unsafe constructs
   - Test error cases where unsafe operations appear outside unsafe blocks
   - Ensure all unsafe syntax variations are properly recognized

2. Type System Tests:
   - Verify type checking rules for unsafe operations
   - Test pointer type conversions and compatibility
   - Ensure unsafe operations are properly typed
   - Test boundary conditions between safe and unsafe code

3. Code Generation Tests:
   - Inspect generated LLVM IR for unsafe operations
   - Verify inline assembly is correctly translated
   - Test pointer arithmetic and memory operations
   - Ensure volatile operations aren't optimized incorrectly

4. Hardware Interaction Tests:
   - Create test harnesses with mock hardware interfaces
   - Verify memory-mapped I/O operations generate correct instructions
   - Test port I/O operations on supported architectures
   - Validate interrupt handling mechanisms

5. FFI Tests:
   - Test calling C functions from Goo code
   - Verify data marshaling between Goo and C types
   - Test dynamic library loading and symbol resolution
   - Ensure ABI compatibility across different platforms

6. Integration Tests:
   - Create a test kernel module using the unsafe features
   - Implement device drivers using the hardware control primitives
   - Test memory management operations in kernel context
   - Verify interrupt handlers function correctly

7. Safety Boundary Tests:
   - Test that unsafe operations can't escape unsafe blocks
   - Verify runtime checks at safe/unsafe boundaries
   - Test compiler warnings for dangerous patterns
   - Ensure safe abstractions around unsafe operations work correctly

8. Performance Tests:
   - Benchmark unsafe operations against safe alternatives
   - Measure overhead of safety checks at boundaries
   - Compare performance with equivalent C code
   - Verify optimizations work correctly with unsafe code

9. LughOS Integration Tests:
   - Test integration with LughOS kernel interfaces
   - Verify hardware control primitives work in kernel context
   - Test interrupt handling in real hardware environment
   - Validate memory management operations with physical memory

tasks.title: Implement GPU Programming Support
tasks.description: Add GPU backend integration with CUDA/OpenCL, implement compute shader compilation targets, GPU memory management, kernel execution, and support for multiple GPU architectures (NVPTX, AMDGPU, SPIR-V, and Metal).
tasks.details:
This task involves extending the compiler to support GPU programming through multiple backends:

1. LLVM Integration for GPU Targets:
   - Extend the LLVM IR code generator to support GPU-specific intrinsics and attributes
   - Implement target-specific code generation for NVPTX, AMDGPU, SPIR-V, and Metal Shading Language
   - Add compilation pipelines for each GPU architecture

2. GPU Memory Management:
   - Implement ownership semantics for GPU memory allocations
   - Create abstractions for device memory, shared memory, and constant memory
   - Add runtime support for memory transfers between host and device
   - Implement memory pool and caching strategies for efficient reuse

3. Kernel Programming Model:
   - Design language extensions for kernel functions with GPU-specific attributes
   - Support thread/block/grid abstractions for CUDA-like programming
   - Implement work-group and work-item abstractions for OpenCL-like programming
   - Add annotations for compute shader entry points

4. Runtime Library:
   - Develop a runtime API for device initialization, context management
   - Implement kernel launch mechanisms with parameter marshaling
   - Create synchronization primitives for GPU execution
   - Add profiling and error handling capabilities

5. Optimization Passes:
   - Implement GPU-specific optimization passes (memory coalescing, shared memory usage)
   - Add vectorization support for GPU SIMD units
   - Create loop transformations specific to GPU execution models
   - Implement automatic kernel fusion where possible

6. Code Examples:
```go
// Example kernel function in Goo
@kernel
func vectorAdd(a, b, c []float32, n int) {
    // Get the global thread ID
    i := blockIdx.x * blockDim.x + threadIdx.x
    if i < n {
        c[i] = a[i] + b[i]
    }
}

// Example kernel launch
func main() {
    // Allocate device memory
    d_a := cuda.Malloc[float32](N)
    d_b := cuda.Malloc[float32](N)
    d_c := cuda.Malloc[float32](N)
    
    // Copy data to device
    cuda.Memcpy(d_a, a, N, cuda.HostToDevice)
    cuda.Memcpy(d_b, b, N, cuda.HostToDevice)
    
    // Launch kernel
    blockSize := 256
    gridSize := (N + blockSize - 1) / blockSize
    vectorAdd<<<gridSize, blockSize>>>(d_a, d_b, d_c, N)
    
    // Copy result back
    cuda.Memcpy(c, d_c, N, cuda.DeviceToHost)
    
    // Free device memory
    cuda.Free(d_a)
    cuda.Free(d_b)
    cuda.Free(d_c)
}
```
tasks.testStrategy:
Testing GPU programming support requires a comprehensive approach across multiple levels:

1. Unit Tests:
   - Create unit tests for each GPU backend component (memory management, kernel launch, etc.)
   - Test GPU type system extensions and validation
   - Verify correct LLVM IR generation for GPU targets using LLVM's verification passes
   - Test ownership semantics for GPU memory allocations

2. Integration Tests:
   - Implement standard GPU algorithm test suite (vector addition, matrix multiplication, etc.)
   - Test cross-device synchronization and memory transfers
   - Verify correct compilation to each target architecture (NVPTX, AMDGPU, SPIR-V, Metal)
   - Test interoperability with existing GPU libraries and frameworks

3. Performance Tests:
   - Benchmark memory transfer speeds between host and device
   - Measure kernel launch overhead and execution time
   - Compare against equivalent CUDA/OpenCL native implementations
   - Profile memory usage patterns and optimization effectiveness

4. Validation Suite:
   - Create a validation suite with examples for each supported GPU feature
   - Test on multiple GPU hardware configurations (NVIDIA, AMD, Intel)
   - Verify correct error handling for out-of-memory, invalid kernel parameters, etc.
   - Test edge cases like large memory allocations and complex kernel launches

5. Automated Testing Infrastructure:
   - Set up CI/CD pipeline with GPU support for automated testing
   - Implement GPU hardware detection and test skipping when hardware is unavailable
   - Create GPU simulators for testing on systems without physical GPUs
   - Add regression tests for previously fixed GPU-related bugs

tasks.title: Implement WebAssembly Support
tasks.description: Add WebAssembly as a compilation target through the LLVM backend, enabling Goo programs to run in browsers and other WASM environments with full interoperability.
tasks.details:
Implement WebAssembly support for the Goo compiler by leveraging the existing LLVM IR code generator and adding WASM-specific functionality:

1. LLVM WASM Target Integration:
   - Configure LLVM backend to target WebAssembly (wasm32)
   - Add WASM-specific optimization passes
   - Implement WASM binary emission and validation

2. Linear Memory Model:
   - Implement WebAssembly's linear memory model
   - Add bounds checking for memory access
   - Create memory management utilities for WASM heap
   - Handle memory growth operations

3. Concurrency Model:
   - Implement cooperative concurrency for single-threaded environments
   - Create event loop integration for asynchronous operations
   - Adapt Goo's concurrency primitives (channels, goroutines) to WASM's execution model
   - Implement task scheduling compatible with browser event loops

4. JavaScript Interoperability:
   - Create bidirectional type marshaling between Goo and JavaScript
   - Implement function call bridging
   - Support for JavaScript callbacks from Goo code
   - Handle primitive types, objects, arrays, and functions
   - Add special handling for DOM objects and browser APIs

5. Runtime Environment Support:
   - Implement browser environment detection and adaptation
   - Add Node.js runtime support
   - Support WASI (WebAssembly System Interface) for non-browser environments
   - Create environment-specific APIs and polyfills

6. DOM Integration:
   - Create type definitions for DOM APIs
   - Implement DOM event handling
   - Add support for DOM manipulation from Goo code
   - Handle browser-specific features and compatibility

7. Build System Integration:
   - Update compiler flags and options for WASM target
   - Create WASM-specific linking and bundling tools
   - Add configuration for different WASM environments
   - Implement source maps for debugging

Example WASM target configuration:
```go
func initWasmTarget(ctx context.Context, llvmModule *llvm.Module) error {
    triple := "wasm32-unknown-unknown"
    llvmModule.SetTarget(triple)
    
    // Configure data layout for WebAssembly
    dataLayout := "e-m:e-p:32:32-i64:64-n32:64-S128"
    llvmModule.SetDataLayout(dataLayout)
    
    // Add WASM-specific attributes
    return nil
}
```

Example JavaScript interop function:
```go
// Export a Goo function to JavaScript
func exportFunction(name string, fn *ast.FuncDecl, module *llvm.Module) error {
    // Mark function as exported in WASM
    llvmFunc := module.GetNamedFunction(name)
    if llvmFunc.IsNil() {
        return fmt.Errorf("function %s not found", name)
    }
    
    llvmFunc.SetLinkage(llvm.ExternalLinkage)
    llvmFunc.AddFunctionAttr(llvm.CreateStringAttribute("wasm-export-name", name))
    
    return nil
}
```
tasks.testStrategy:
To verify the WebAssembly support implementation:

1. Unit Testing:
   - Create unit tests for WASM-specific code generation
   - Test memory model implementation with boundary cases
   - Verify type marshaling between JavaScript and Goo types
   - Test concurrency primitives in WASM environment

2. Integration Testing:
   - Compile simple Goo programs to WASM and verify correct execution
   - Test interoperability with JavaScript by calling Goo functions from JS and vice versa
   - Verify DOM manipulation works correctly in browser environments
   - Test event handling and asynchronous operations

3. Environment Testing:
   - Test in multiple browsers (Chrome, Firefox, Safari, Edge)
   - Verify functionality in Node.js environment
   - Test WASI compatibility in appropriate runtimes
   - Verify performance characteristics across environments

4. Benchmark Suite:
   - Create benchmarks comparing WASM performance to native code
   - Measure memory usage and garbage collection behavior
   - Test startup time and code size optimization
   - Compare performance of different WASM optimization levels

5. Example Applications:
   - Build a small web application using Goo compiled to WASM
   - Create a Node.js CLI tool using WASM
   - Implement a browser game or interactive visualization
   - Test a server-side component using WASI

6. Automated Testing:
   - Set up CI/CD pipeline for WASM target testing
   - Create browser automation tests for DOM integration
   - Implement cross-environment test suite
   - Add regression tests for all major features

Test command examples:
```bash
# Compile test program to WASM
goo build --target=wasm32 -o test.wasm test.goo

# Run WASM tests in Node.js environment
node wasm_test_runner.js test.wasm

# Run browser tests with headless Chrome
karma start --browsers ChromeHeadless
```

tasks.title: Implement Standard Library Foundation
tasks.description: Create core modules (io, fmt, strings), collections (vec, map, set), network programming with channel patterns, OS interfaces, and essential utilities that provide a solid foundation for Goo applications.
tasks.testStrategy:
To verify the correct implementation of the Standard Library Foundation, follow these testing steps:

1. Unit Testing:
   - Create comprehensive unit tests for each module and function
   - Test both normal operation and error conditions
   - Verify that ownership semantics are correctly implemented
   - Example for testing the `strings` package:
     ```go
     func TestStringSplit() {
         result := strings.Split("a,b,c", ",")
         assert.Equal([]string{"a", "b", "c"}, result)
         
         result = strings.Split("abc", ",")
         assert.Equal([]string{"abc"}, result)
         
         result = strings.Split("", ",")
         assert.Equal([]string{""}, result)
     }
     ```

2. Integration Testing:
   - Create test programs that use multiple standard library components together
   - Verify that components interact correctly
   - Test for memory leaks and ownership violations
   - Example integration test:
     ```go
     func TestFileReadAndParse() {
         // Create a temporary file
         file, err := os.Create("test.txt")
         assert.NoError(err)
         
         // Write to the file
         _, err = file.Write([]byte("line1\nline2\nline3"))
         assert.NoError(err)
         file.Close()
         
         // Read the file
         content, err := os.ReadFile("test.txt")
         assert.NoError(err)
         
         // Parse the content
         lines := strings.Split(string(content), "\n")
         assert.Equal([]string{"line1", "line2", "line3"}, lines)
         
         // Clean up
         os.Remove("test.txt")
     }
     ```

3. Performance Testing:
   - Benchmark critical operations in each module
   - Compare performance against similar operations in Go
   - Ensure collections perform efficiently for various sizes
   - Example benchmark:
     ```go
     func BenchmarkVecPush() {
         v := vec.New[int]()
         for i := 0; i < 1000000; i++ {
             v.Push(i)
         }
     }
     ```

4. Memory Safety Testing:
   - Verify that memory is properly managed according to Goo's ownership rules
   - Test for memory leaks using tools like Valgrind
   - Ensure that borrowed references don't outlive their owners
   - Example memory safety test:
     ```go
     func TestMapOwnership() {
         m := map.New[string, vec.Vec[int]]()
         
         // Create and insert a vector
         v := vec.New[int]()
         v.Push(1)
         v.Push(2)
         
         // Transfer ownership to the map
         m.Insert("key", v)
         
         // v should no longer be usable if ownership was transferred
         // This should cause a compile-time error in Goo
         // v.Push(3) // Should not compile
         
         // Get a borrowed reference
         borrowed := m.GetRef("key")
         assert.Equal(2, borrowed.Len())
         
         // Borrowed reference should not allow mutation
         // borrowed.Push(3) // Should not compile
     }
     ```

5. Error Handling Testing:
   - Verify that error unions are correctly implemented
   - Test error propagation across module boundaries
   - Ensure that nullable types are handled properly
   - Example error handling test:
     ```go
     func TestFileOpenError() {
         // Try to open a non-existent file
         _, err := os.Open("nonexistent.txt")
         
         // Check that we got an error
         assert.Error(err)
         
         // Test error handling with match statement
         match err {
             case os.ErrNotExist:
                 // Expected case
                 assert.True(true)
             default:
                 assert.Fail("Expected ErrNotExist")
         }
     }
     ```

6. Cross-Platform Testing:
   - Test the standard library on multiple platforms (Linux, macOS, Windows)
   - Verify that OS-specific functionality works correctly
   - Ensure consistent behavior across platforms where appropriate
   - Document platform-specific differences

7. Documentation Testing:
   - Verify that all examples in the documentation compile and run correctly
   - Test that the documentation is complete for all public APIs
   - Ensure that ownership and error handling patterns are clearly documented

8. End-to-End Testing:
   - Create sample applications that use the standard library extensively
   - Verify that these applications work correctly
   - Example applications could include:
     - A simple HTTP server
     - A file processing utility
     - A network client/server application using channel patterns

tasks.title: Implement Production Tooling
tasks.description: Create a comprehensive toolchain for Goo language development and deployment, including package manager, build system, debugger, profiler, LSP support, formatter, and documentation generator.
tasks.details:
Implement a complete production-ready toolchain for the Goo programming language that enhances developer productivity and supports enterprise-grade development workflows:

1. Package Manager (Goo Cargo):
   - Implement dependency resolution and version management
   - Support for publishing and consuming packages
   - Lockfile generation for reproducible builds
   - Package manifest format:
   ```
   # goo.toml
   [package]
   name = "my_package"
   version = "0.1.0"
   authors = ["developer@example.com"]
   
   [dependencies]
   http = "1.2.0"
   json = { version = "0.9.1", features = ["validation"] }
   ```
   - Command-line interface for package operations:
   ```
   goo add http@1.2.0
   goo update
   goo publish
   ```

2. Build System Improvements:
   - Incremental compilation support
   - Parallel build pipeline
   - Cross-compilation targets
   - Custom build scripts
   - Optimization level configuration
   - Build profiles (debug, release, test)
   - Integration with the LLVM toolchain

3. Debugger Support:
   - DWARF debug information generation
   - Integration with GDB/LLDB
   - Custom debug visualizers for Goo types
   - Breakpoint management
   - Variable inspection and modification
   - Stack trace navigation

4. Profiler Integration:
   - CPU profiling with flamegraph generation
   - Memory allocation tracking
   - Heap analysis tools
   - I/O and network profiling
   - Integration with existing profiling tools (perf, VTune)

5. Language Server Protocol (LSP) Implementation:
   - Code completion
   - Go-to-definition
   - Find references
   - Hover documentation
   - Signature help
   - Diagnostics reporting
   - Code actions and quick fixes
   - Implement the server component:
   ```go
   func handleTextDocumentCompletion(params lsp.CompletionParams) []lsp.CompletionItem {
       // Parse file at params.TextDocument.URI
       // Find completion items at params.Position
       // Return appropriate completion items
   }
   ```

6. Code Formatter (goo fmt):
   - Consistent code style enforcement
   - Configurable formatting rules
   - Editor integration
   - Pre-commit hook support
   - Batch formatting for projects

7. Documentation Generator:
   - Extract documentation from code comments
   - Generate HTML/Markdown documentation
   - Support for code examples
   - API reference generation
   - Integration with package repository

8. Toolchain Integration:
   - Single entry point command (`goo`) with subcommands
   - Consistent configuration across tools
   - Plugin architecture for extensibility
   - CI/CD integration helpers
   - Containerization support

Implementation should focus on developer experience while maintaining compatibility with existing Go tooling where appropriate. The toolchain should be designed with extensibility in mind to accommodate future language evolution.
tasks.testStrategy:
Testing for the production tooling will require a multi-faceted approach to ensure all components work correctly individually and together:

1. Package Manager Testing:
   - Create test packages with various dependency structures
   - Verify correct resolution of complex dependency graphs
   - Test version conflict resolution
   - Validate lockfile generation and reproducibility
   - Test package publishing and consumption workflows
   - Verify handling of circular dependencies

2. Build System Testing:
   - Measure and verify incremental build performance
   - Test cross-compilation to multiple targets
   - Verify correct application of optimization levels
   - Test parallel build correctness with race conditions
   - Validate build caching mechanisms

3. Debugger Support Testing:
   - Verify correct DWARF information generation
   - Test integration with GDB/LLDB using sample programs
   - Validate breakpoint functionality
   - Test variable inspection for all Goo types
   - Verify stack trace accuracy

4. Profiler Testing:
   - Create benchmark programs with known performance characteristics
   - Validate profiler output against expected hotspots
   - Test memory allocation tracking accuracy
   - Verify profiler overhead is within acceptable limits
   - Test integration with external profiling tools

5. LSP Implementation Testing:
   - Create test suite covering all LSP features
   - Test against multiple editor integrations (VS Code, Vim, Emacs)
   - Verify performance with large codebases
   - Test error handling and recovery
   - Validate diagnostic accuracy

6. Formatter Testing:
   - Create test cases covering all syntax constructs
   - Verify idempotence (formatting twice produces same result)
   - Test handling of malformed input
   - Verify configuration options work as expected
   - Benchmark performance on large files

7. Documentation Generator Testing:
   - Test extraction of documentation from various comment styles
   - Verify generated documentation accuracy
   - Test handling of code examples
   - Validate cross-references and links
   - Test with multilingual documentation

8. Integration Testing:
   - Create end-to-end workflows that use multiple tools
   - Test command-line interface consistency
   - Verify error reporting is consistent across tools
   - Test plugin architecture with sample extensions
   - Validate CI/CD integration

9. User Acceptance Testing:
   - Recruit early adopters to use the toolchain
   - Collect feedback on usability issues
   - Measure productivity improvements
   - Identify missing features or pain points

Each component should have both unit tests and integration tests, with automated test suites that can be run as part of CI/CD pipelines.

tasks.title: Implement LughOS Integration Framework
tasks.description: Create a freestanding runtime with no_std support, hardware abstraction layer (HAL), boot sequence, and kernel services architecture to enable Goo language integration with embedded and OS development.
tasks.details:
This task involves implementing the core components needed for LughOS integration, allowing Goo to be used for operating system and embedded systems development:

1. Freestanding Runtime with no_std Support:
   - Implement a minimal runtime that can operate without the standard library
   - Create core memory primitives that work without an underlying OS
   - Develop bare-metal allocation strategies (bump allocator, free list allocator)
   - Implement core data structures needed for kernel development

2. Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL):
   - Define trait interfaces for hardware components (CPU, MMU, timers, interrupts)
   - Implement architecture-specific HAL modules (starting with x86_64)
   - Create memory-mapped I/O abstractions
   - Develop device driver interfaces and registration system

3. Boot Sequence and Initialization:
   - Implement multiboot2-compatible entry point
   - Create early initialization routines (stack setup, BSS clearing)
   - Develop CPU feature detection and initialization
   - Implement platform detection and hardware enumeration

4. Interrupt Descriptor Table (IDT) Management:
   - Create IDT data structures and setup routines
   - Implement interrupt handler registration system
   - Develop exception handlers for CPU faults
   - Add support for custom interrupt handlers

5. Memory Management Unit (MMU) Control:
   - Implement page table manipulation functions
   - Create virtual memory mapping abstractions
   - Develop memory protection mechanisms
   - Implement TLB management functions

6. Kernel Services Architecture:
   - Design modular kernel service interfaces
   - Implement inter-service communication mechanisms
   - Create service registration and discovery system
   - Develop service lifecycle management

7. Capability-Based Security System:
   - Implement capability data structures and validation
   - Create capability delegation and revocation mechanisms
   - Develop object capability references
   - Implement capability-based access control

8. Example Microkernel Implementation:
   - Create a minimal working microkernel using the framework
   - Implement process isolation and scheduling
   - Add IPC mechanisms (message passing, shared memory)
   - Develop a simple device driver model

The implementation should leverage Goo's unsafe blocks and hardware control primitives to interact directly with hardware while maintaining safety guarantees in non-unsafe code. The framework should be designed to be modular, allowing developers to use only the components they need for their specific OS or embedded project.
tasks.testStrategy:
Testing this low-level framework requires a combination of unit tests, integration tests, and hardware-based verification:

1. Unit Testing:
   - Create unit tests for individual components using a hardware emulation layer
   - Test memory management primitives with various allocation patterns
   - Verify IDT setup and interrupt handling with simulated interrupts
   - Test capability validation and access control logic

2. Integration Testing:
   - Develop integration tests that verify interactions between subsystems
   - Test boot sequence in emulated environment (QEMU)
   - Verify HAL abstractions work correctly across different simulated hardware
   - Test kernel service interactions and lifecycle management

3. Hardware Testing:
   - Create a test suite that runs on actual hardware (x86_64 systems initially)
   - Verify interrupt handling with hardware-triggered interrupts
   - Test MMU operations by mapping/unmapping memory and verifying access
   - Validate hardware detection and initialization on multiple platforms

4. Microkernel Verification:
   - Boot the example microkernel in both emulated and real hardware
   - Verify process isolation by attempting to access memory across process boundaries
   - Test IPC mechanisms with multi-process communication scenarios
   - Measure performance metrics for context switching and memory operations

5. Automated Testing Infrastructure:
   - Set up CI/CD pipeline with QEMU-based testing
   - Create automated test reports for hardware compatibility
   - Implement fault injection testing to verify error handling
   - Develop stress tests for memory management and interrupt handling

6. Documentation Verification:
   - Ensure all public APIs are documented with examples
   - Verify that example code in documentation compiles and runs
   - Create and test tutorials for creating custom HAL implementations
   - Validate that the microkernel example is fully documented and functional

The testing should verify both functional correctness and performance characteristics, ensuring the framework is suitable for real-world OS development.
